rationale: The cited paper is used in the main text to ground the really new product learning literature, not just in a passing list. The 2009 article specifically invokes Moreau, Lehmann, and Markman (2001) as evidence that analogy-based learning can improve comprehension/acceptance of new products and can have limits for experienced consumers. These points help motivate the citing paper's core positioning of imagination-focused visualization as an alternative way to improve RNP evaluation.
relation_description: The citing paper treats the cited paper as prior conceptual and empirical groundwork on how consumers learn about really new products. It builds its problem framing around those findings to justify testing imagination-focused visualization as a different learning intervention.
